text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Britain and Northern Ireland’s Olympians produced a series of triumphs that will live long in the memory

And just like that, another Summer Olympics has come and gone. TheCity of Lights extinguished its Olympic flame, drawing to a close a Games filled with historic comebacks, memorable moments, and final send-offs for sporting legends.

While Team GB may be looking back on Paris 2024 with a touch of frustration after theirlowest return of gold medals in 20 years, Britain and Northern Ireland’s Olympians still produced a series of triumphs that will live long in the memory.

One of those, which will likely find itself jammed into Team GB sporting montages for years to come, sawKeely Hodgkinson carry the hopes of a nationto end her 800 metres final at Stade de France with a crown adorned on her head.

The Mancunian stormed down the final straight to replicate her hero Dame Kelly Holmes’ Athens 2004 run, and collect Team GB’s solitary gold from the athletics meet.

Outside the stadium, Team GB found more luck withAlex Yee who produced a stunning finishto the men’s triathlon – delayed by a day due to a polluted Seine – to emulate Alistair Brownlee as Britain’s second Olympic champion in the sport.

Tom Pidcock’s dramatic comebackfrom a punctured tire where he made up a 40 second deficit to pip Frenchman Victor Koretzky in the mountain bike also proved memorable. Booed by the home crowd, Pidcock broke French hearts to secure gold for Team GB.

Over a week later, when British hopes threatened to fizzle out after days without a top prize,Ellie Aldridge came to the rescueto take home Team GB’s first kitesurfing gold medal

But while there were several highs to paper over the cracks of underperforming Team GB, there was a succession of nearly-moments that denied written-in-the-stars victories.

Katarina Johnson-Thompson looked in prime position to clinch goldafter the first day of the heptathlon, having fought back from injury and setback that has plagued her career. But the silver still meant everything to the athlete who had overcome her demons as she finally reached the podium at her fourth Olympics.

And after “three years of hell”, and attempting suicide in 2021, a Matthew Hudson-Smith victory in the men’s 400m final looked realistic as he stormed into the home straight – 100 years after Eric Liddle first won the race for GB.

But a lung-bursting finish from American Quincy Hall sawHudson-Smith edged out by the razor-thin marginof four hundredths of a second. But it didn’t make his comeback any the less heartwarming.

Josh Kerr’s scripted two-man 1500 metres showdown with rival Jakob Ingebrigtsenwas spoiled when Grant Hocker shocked everyone to beat both to the line. But the proud Scotsman himself said he had nothing more to give.

The Games sawAndy Murray bid goodbye to tennisafter his hopes of a medal-winning swansong evaporated alongside Dan Evans in the men’s doubles with defeat to American pair Taylor Fritz and Tommy Paul.

The Team GB duo entertained Olympic crowds with two dramatic winning matches at Roland Garros, leaving Murray satisfied after finishing his career on his terms.

Among the highs there were also lows, with the Games enduring its fair-share of controversy, notably with astill rumbling gender eligibility row.

Those involved, Chinese Taipei boxer Lin Yu Ting andAlgerian Imane Khelif each won their weight divisions, having been cleared to compete by the International Olympic Committee (IOC) despite International Boxing Association (IBA) disqualifying them having said they failed gender eligibility tests. The IBA has since been expelled over financial and corruption concerns by the IOC.

But if the Olympics belonged to any individual, it was without a doubtTeam USA’s Simone Bileswho – after being forced to withdraw from the pandemic-delayed Tokyo 2020 with gymnastic mental block ‘the twisties’ – roared back to produce one of the great career comebacks. At 27 she became the oldest ever all-around champion, while picking up the vault and team titles.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

title: The moments we won't forget from the Paris 2024 Olympics
author: Christian Oliver
description: Britain and Northern Ireland’s Olympians produced a series of triumphs that will live long in the memory
